content: Big-O complexity hierarchy from fastest to slowest: O(1) constant < O(log n) logarithmic < O(n) linear < O(n log n) linearithmic < O(n^2) quadratic < O(2^n) exponential < O(n!) factorial. For n=1,000,000: O(n log n) is ~20M operations while O(n^2) is 1 trillion.

Amortized analysis averages worst-case over a sequence of operations. Dynamic array append is O(1) amortized despite O(n) worst-case during resize. The accounting method assigns $3 per insert: $1 for placement, $1 for future move of this element, $1 for moving an existing element during doubling.

Hash tables provide O(1) average-case lookup, insert, and delete with a good hash function. Load factor alpha = n/m (elements/buckets) should stay below 0.75 for chaining and 0.5 for open addressing. Resizing doubles the table and rehashes all elements in O(n) amortized O(1).

Hash collision resolution: Chaining uses linked lists at each bucket (degrades to O(n) if all collide, O(1+alpha) average). Open addressing probes for empty slots: linear probing (cache-friendly but clustering), quadratic probing (reduces clustering), double hashing (best distribution, h1(k)+i*h2(k)).

Binary search on a sorted array runs in O(log n) time by halving the search space each iteration. For n=1,000,000, binary search requires at most ceil(log2(1000000))=20 comparisons. The iterative version uses lo=0, hi=n-1, mid=(lo+hi)//2 and is preferred over recursive to avoid stack overflow.

Variants of binary search: lower_bound (first element >= target), upper_bound (first element > target), search on rotated array (modified condition checking which half is sorted), and binary search on answer (minimize/maximize a value satisfying a monotonic predicate).

BFS explores a graph level by level using a queue, finding shortest paths in unweighted graphs in O(V+E) time and O(V) space. DFS explores as deep as possible using a stack (or recursion), also O(V+E) time. BFS is optimal for shortest path; DFS is better for topological sort and cycle detection.

Dijkstra's algorithm finds shortest paths from a source in O((V+E) log V) with a min-heap. It fails with negative edges. Bellman-Ford handles negative edges in O(VE) and detects negative cycles. A* uses heuristic h(n) to guide search: f(n)=g(n)+h(n), optimal when h is admissible (never overestimates).

Dynamic programming requires two properties: overlapping subproblems (same subproblems recur) and optimal substructure (optimal solution contains optimal subsolutions). Top-down (memoization) caches recursive results; bottom-up (tabulation) fills a table iteratively. DP reduces exponential brute force to polynomial time.

Classic DP problems and complexities: Fibonacci O(n), Longest Common Subsequence O(mn), 0/1 Knapsack O(nW), Edit Distance O(mn), Coin Change O(nS), Longest Increasing Subsequence O(n log n) with patience sorting, Matrix Chain Multiplication O(n^3).

Greedy algorithms make locally optimal choices that lead to a globally optimal solution when the problem has the matroid property (hereditary + exchange). Examples: Huffman coding (optimal prefix codes), Kruskal/Prim MST, Activity Selection, and Dijkstra (non-negative weights). Greedy fails for 0/1 Knapsack but works for fractional.

Merge sort divides the array in half recursively and merges sorted halves in O(n log n) time guaranteed, with O(n) auxiliary space. It is stable (preserves relative order of equal elements). Quick sort averages O(n log n) with O(log n) space but degrades to O(n^2) worst-case; randomized pivot prevents this.

Counting sort runs in O(n+k) where k is the range of values, suitable when k=O(n). Radix sort processes digits from least to most significant using a stable sort, running in O(d*(n+k)) where d is the number of digits. Both are non-comparison sorts that break the O(n log n) comparison lower bound.

A trie (prefix tree) stores strings character by character in a tree structure with O(m) lookup where m is the string length, independent of the number of stored strings. Each node has up to 26 children (for lowercase English). Tries enable prefix-based autocomplete, spell checking, and IP routing (longest prefix match).

Compressed tries (radix trees) merge chains of single-child nodes into one edge, reducing space from O(ALPHABET*n*m) to O(n*m). Patricia tries are binary radix trees used in IP routing tables. Ternary search tries balance memory (3 pointers per node) vs. speed for dictionary applications.

A binary heap is a complete binary tree stored in an array where parent at index i has children at 2i+1 and 2i+2. Insert and extract-min/max are O(log n). Heapify (build heap from array) is O(n). Priority queues use heaps for task scheduling, Dijkstra's algorithm, and merge k sorted lists.

Fibonacci heaps provide O(1) amortized insert, decrease-key, and merge, with O(log n) extract-min. This makes Dijkstra O(V log V + E) instead of O((V+E) log V). In practice, binary heaps are faster due to lower constant factors and better cache behavior despite worse theoretical bounds.

Bloom filters test set membership probabilistically using k hash functions mapping to m bits. False positive rate is (1 - e^(-kn/m))^k where n is the number of elements. For 1% false positive rate with 1M elements, use m=9.6M bits (1.2 MB) with k=7 hash functions. No false negatives ever occur.

Counting Bloom filters replace bits with counters (typically 4-bit) to support deletion at the cost of 4x space. Cuckoo filters achieve lower false positive rates than Bloom filters at the same space with O(1) lookup and support deletion. For 1M items at 1% FPR, a Cuckoo filter uses ~7.2 bits per item.

Red-black trees guarantee O(log n) search, insert, and delete with at most 2 rotations per insert and 3 per delete. The height is at most 2*log2(n+1). AVL trees are more strictly balanced (heights differ by at most 1) giving faster lookups but slower inserts/deletes due to more rotations.

B-trees are self-balancing trees optimized for disk I/O with branching factor b. A B-tree of order m has at most m children per node. Search is O(log_b(n)) disk reads. B+ trees store all data in leaves with linked-list connections, enabling efficient range queries. Databases use B+ trees with typical order 100-1000.

Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) supports union and find operations in O(alpha(n)) amortized time with path compression and union by rank, where alpha is the inverse Ackermann function (effectively constant, <5 for any practical n). Used in Kruskal's MST algorithm and detecting connected components.

Segment trees support range queries (sum, min, max) and point updates in O(log n) each, built in O(n). Lazy propagation enables range updates in O(log n). Fenwick trees (Binary Indexed Trees) use O(n) space and O(log n) for prefix sum queries and point updates with simpler implementation than segment trees.

Topological sort orders vertices of a DAG such that for every edge (u,v), u comes before v. Kahn's algorithm uses BFS with in-degree tracking in O(V+E). It detects cycles (not all vertices processed). Applications include task scheduling, build systems, course prerequisites, and dependency resolution.

The sliding window technique solves subarray/substring problems in O(n) by maintaining a window with two pointers. Fixed window: compute first window, then slide (add right, remove left). Variable window: expand right until condition met, shrink left to optimize. Examples: maximum sum subarray of size k, longest substring without repeats.
